topic: Map Interface UI — Rich Journey View
long_term_goal: Daniel opens the trip map and immediately understands the full journey — each leg's drive time and distance is visible without clicking, mandatory break points are suggested, and a discovery layer shows castles, lakes, and mountains within reach of the route.
sprint_questions: Can we show drive time and distance for each leg on the route line without cluttering the map?
Can we surface break stop opportunities and POIs along the route without introducing a new data dependency beyond Mapbox?
Will Daniel be able to confirm at a glance that every leg is within the 5-hour limit?
key_question_answered: Can we show drive time and distance for each leg without cluttering the map? Yes — segment data already exists in the Directions API response; a Mapbox symbol layer at segment midpoints renders it cleanly.
key_decision: Rebuilt the trip viewer as a desktop two-panel layout (300px itinerary sidebar + full-height Mapbox outdoors map) with colour-coded segment time labels on the route line and a gated POI discovery toggle.
elias_dissent: Two concerns: (1) POI data quality on rural European routes via queryRenderedFeatures is inconsistent — POI layer must be validated against the actual Cork route before shipping; (2) Hannah's mobile experience is being deferred to Sprint 004, which Elias accepted but flagged as a product gap.
next_action: Run the implementation sprint: build two-panel layout, segment labels, colour coding, and Mapbox outdoors style switch. Gate POI layer on data quality validation against the actual Cork route.
open_questions: Which Mapbox outdoors style layer IDs surface castles, viewpoints, and natural landmarks for European routes?
Does queryRenderedFeatures return meaningful POIs at the zoom level needed to view the full Cork route?
How does the sidebar overflow-y interact with the existing trip page layout structure?
